description: Roll the dual-readership framing into v0.1.14: document and convention it first, then cut the release.

items.before:
The worklist-history feature serves two readers: humans during active triage of items, and future agents grepping for forensic context after the original session is gone. Neither place in the codebase currently makes this explicit:

- `app/__shell/conventions.md` is silent on how to write item prose. An agent drafts `before`/`after` based on whatever feels right in the moment, which often leans toward the immediate human reader and skips alternatives considered.
- `docs/worklist-history.md` describes the mechanism (flow, phases, routes, UI) but never says who the artifact is FOR. A reader doesn't learn why specificity in the prose earns its keep.

Reverts in this very session — the commit-correlation feature, the per-item lifecycle filter — are now invisible in the squashed git log; their only trace was in snapshot prose that we discarded as dev-era artifact. A future agent wondering 'did we consider audit-trail correlation?' has worse retrieval than they should.
items.after:
Two-file change:

(1) `docs/worklist-history.md` — add a short 'Who reads this' section near the top of the doc, capturing the dual-readership story. Humans use the artifact during active triage; agents lean on it later as forensic audit trail. The implication for prose authors: write `before`/`after` for the long-tail reader, name alternatives considered (not just the chosen path), and assume the agent grepping months from now does not have access to the conversation that produced the item.

(2) `app/__shell/conventions.md` — under the worklist section (near 'When to route through the worklist'), add a brief authorship rule: 'Write item prose with the future-agent reader in mind. Name alternatives considered, not just the chosen path. The agent who reads this snapshot months from now does not have the conversation that produced it — the worklist history is their only retrieval.' Conventions get auto-loaded into every agent's context, so this rule actively shapes how items get drafted from this commit forward.

Alternatives considered: (a) doc-only change, no convention update — rejected because the doc is reference material, not behavioral guidance; without the convention line, the rule doesn't propagate. (b) Convention-only, no doc update — rejected because the convention rule cites a framing that's then nowhere expanded. (c) Single combined paragraph in just one file — rejected because the two readers (agent loading conventions, human reading the doc) want different framings of the same point.

Commit message: 'docs: frame worklist-history as agent-forensic audit trail'.

items.before: Last release was v0.1.13 (commit cad4262). Since then we've landed the worklist-history feature — snapshot capture + .md changelog + augmented /__worklist + the History expander + the rename-aware hook + the dual-readership framing rolled into this same release — squashed into 879f9cd (code), 15fe82b (docs), and whatever commit the framing item produces. No version bump yet; the on-disk binary still identifies as 0.1.13.
items.after:
Standard release-cut per the conventions.md recipe:

1. Bump version `0.1.13` → `0.1.14` in `src-tauri/Cargo.toml` and `src-tauri/tauri.conf.json`.
2. `cargo build` to refresh `Cargo.lock`.
3. Commit message: `Release v0.1.14`.
4. Create local tag `v0.1.14` at the release commit.

Alternatives considered: patch bump vs minor bump (0.2.0). Patch chosen because xmlui-desktop is still pre-1.0; minor/patch boundaries are loose in this range, and consistency with prior 0.1.x cadence trumps semver strictness.

Pushing the commits (agent-tools drawer push button), pushing the tag (`git push origin v0.1.14`), and dispatching `.github/workflows/build.yml` from the GitHub Actions UI are user-driven steps after the worklist items land.
